title: Why the Demonstration in Chennai on 5 January?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All nine source pages were checked for rainfall figures, flood chronology, personal accounts, the ministers' praise-board wording, quoted press investigations, and the closing signature. Source-visible OCR damage such as “கருணையின் பிறப்பிடமே” and “விடியல் தந்த வெற்றித் திருமகளே” was restored only in the reading copy. The printed chapter ends with M.K.'s signature but contains no closing date, so none has been inferred.
paragraphs: At the meeting held under my leadership at the DMK headquarters on 23 December 2015, a resolution was adopted and announced: to emphasise that the ruling AIADMK Government must accept responsibility for the destruction and losses caused by the floods in Tamil Nadu at the beginning of December, a protest demonstration would be held under my leadership in Chennai at about 10 a.m. on Tuesday, 5 January 2016.

Our District Secretary Ma. Subramanian sought police permission to hold it in Saidapet, which had been severely affected by the floods. The police replied that demonstrations in Chennai district could be held only at one of three designated locations. It has therefore been decided to conduct the protest near the Government Guest House at Chepauk, Chennai.

Although the location falls within Chennai West District, the South, West, East and North Chennai districts, all affected by the torrential rain and flooding, are jointly making excellent arrangements. The respective District Secretaries of Kanchipuram North and South and Tiruvallur South and North are also arranging for thousands of affected brethren and members of the public from those districts to participate.
For the Kazhagam, the year 2016 begins with a protest demonstration. Why this protest?

Statistics say Chennai experienced rain unseen in a hundred years: 13.6 cm on 9 November; heavy rain again on 13, 16 and 23 November; a maximum of 24.6 cm on 16 November; 14 cm on the 23rd; and 20 cm on each of two consecutive days at the end of November. November alone received 119.73 cm.

What disasters did the floods cause? Who must tell us, if not the Government? How many died? How many houses were destroyed? How many businesses were ruined? What was the monetary loss? Has the Government supplied any list? How many flood-hit places did the Chief Minister visit? Which areas did she visit? How many statements did she issue? How often did she meet the press? Was any information provided?

Only once did Jayalalithaa address the people, through WhatsApp. In it, the Chief Minister delivered a message of comfort to all the people of Tamil Nadu. When the telephone rang at home and one answered, there was Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's speech! Was it truly Jayalalithaa speaking, or was someone else speaking in her voice? In that speech, did she not promise the affected people not to worry because she would look after them all? What did she do?

On one day alone, she went to console people in her constituency. Even then she did not address those affected as “People of Tamil Nadu” or “blood of my blood.” Her outlook remained electoral: at every gathering, she called them only “respected voters.” Every newspaper prominently reported it. Did she offer any explanation? None at all!

Because of the continuous rain, schools and colleges were closed for twenty-two days and examinations postponed. Chennai's roads were submerged. Water accumulated on railway tracks and train services stopped. The airport itself closed. People from outside Chennai, unable to return home, stayed in railway stations. With hotels closed, there was nothing to eat. People suffered without even milk and bread for their children. There was no electricity for four or five consecutive days. Telephones did not work. The people's hardship was hardship beyond words! There was no limit to the suffering of working-class and middle-class people. But was there a Government in Tamil Nadu capable of sharing their grief?

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, who obtained votes during the election by asking, “Will you do it? Will you do it?”, did not come in person to offer comfort. Instead, AIADMK workers were intent on forcibly pasting stickers bearing “Amma's” portrait even on relief materials supplied by voluntary organisations. In particular, sandbags were stacked to prevent water entering the Jaya Television office, and a fire engine stood ready at its entrance.

The entire ground floor of one of my secretaries' homes filled with water. He and his wife tried to stay upstairs, but could not come down and had nothing to eat. I eventually sent one of my security personnel to rescue them, after which they stayed in a hotel for four or five days. One of my doctors escaped from his house and spent four or five days at a friend's home outside Chennai.

Water entered the homes of many friends who work at Anna Arivalayam, causing each of them personal losses of many thousands of rupees. Several of my security personnel went to housing colonies and rescued people struggling for their lives; they later came and told me everything directly. People who had bought cars only after great difficulty saw them submerged and now do not know what to do, despite having insurance.

We must all bow our heads before the immeasurable service rendered amid this cruelty by voluntary organisations, especially young people. They worked without concern for their own lives. Yet Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, who had asked “Will you do it? Will you do it?” to win votes, did not come in person to offer comfort. Instead, AIADMK workers focused on forcibly pasting stickers bearing “Amma's” portrait even on relief supplies provided by voluntary organisations. Sandbags were stacked to keep water out of the Jaya Television office, and a fire engine stood ready at its entrance.

Meanwhile, water filled the entrance of our house and surrounded the car, until the Area Secretary used a machine to remove it. For several days, newspapers could not even be published. When they reappeared two days later, they carried accounts of everything people in the flood-hit areas had suffered, with scenes of people carrying infants through the streets as though wading across a river.

Who was responsible for all this suffering and grief? Was nature alone responsible? If the Government had authorised the opening of Chembarambakkam Lake at the proper time, would it have been necessary to release such an enormous quantity all at once on 1 December and drown Chennai?

While the people of Chennai were suffering in this manner, what did the Chief Minister do, even if she would not come forward to extend a helping hand? She came to the Secretariat and collected relief-fund cheques from the wealthy and industrialists. She opened something by video conference and arranged to have the photographs appear in newspapers.

How many arrangements were made merely for her arrival? Please come and look at the streets along her route in Chennai. Do you know how many welcome banners there are? Look at just a few words on the flex boards erected today.
There is a large board headed “Birthplace of compassion.” Do you know who put it up? The Revenue Department.

“Ocean of mercy,” says a large board sponsored by Minister Velumani. Another says, “Heir to the great philanthropist,” again from Minister Velumani. Minister Thangamani has erected one calling her “Lamp of the poor.” Minister Vaithilingam has put up boards saying “Guardian deity of the Kazhagam” and “Breath of our lives.”

Other Ministers have placed boards proclaiming: “Address of victory,” “Deity of our clan,” “Embodiment of wisdom, hand of strength,” “Dawn of deliverance for farmers,” “Leader of the poor,” “Miraculous gift,” “Dawn 555, maiden of victory,” “Birthplace of generosity,” “Our loving mother who sowed the seeds of greenness,” “Abode of triumph,” and “Mother Cauvery.” Anyone can go even today and see them arranged in rows, leaving people scarcely enough room to walk.

Do you know what board the Mayor of Chennai has erected? A huge advertisement bears the long heading: “Life-breath of truth! Your breath across the world! Inspiration of the Corporation! Our life!” Has he not committed more wrongs than the others? That is why his board is larger!

While people cry that their livelihoods have vanished, there are so many welcome boards at the Chief Minister's doorstep. Decorative arches are not confined to her house. From the Secretariat in Chennai, she opened a Collectorate built in a low-lying part of Tiruppur. Yet flex boards carrying Jayalalithaa's portrait extended for two kilometres through Tiruppur!
At the same time, several weeklies have written in detail about the true face of these rulers. Here are portions of an article in this week's *Nakkheeran* under the title “The Concealed Death Toll”:

> “Across the State, voices echo that the Tamil Nadu Government is concealing events from the rain and floods that left unforgettable, deep scars in people's minds and is issuing false statistics. IAS officers working in areas affected by the natural disaster say that Jayalalithaa's request to the Union Government for ₹26,000 crore in flood relief, even before a complete assessment of Tamil Nadu's losses had been compiled, clearly shows that the Tamil Nadu Government has not acted transparently in flood-relief work. > > “Although advance warning was given that rain and flooding were coming, the State Government paid no attention. Across Tamil Nadu, people have begun openly saying that the Jayalalithaa Government made no effort to protect them from the destructive flood.”
Director Thangar Bachan told *Puthiya Thalaimurai*:

> “My house is in Ekkatuthangal. Twenty-four friends from our street and I stayed on the upper floor of our house for eight days. We kept hoping help might arrive by helicopter or boat. I lost my agricultural land, two cars and two bikes. More than all those possessions, I lost four friends. They know the capacity of Chembarambakkam Lake. Do they not even know to release surplus water according to the inflow? > > “People were already struggling in rainwater. Without telling them, without warning, they suddenly opened the lake in the middle of the night. That is why the damage was so great. However hard we try, we cannot change the present rulers. We must therefore choose the coming rulers correctly.”
*Junior Vikatan* wrote:

> “As early as the morning of 28 November, Public Works Department officials gave the Chief Secretary a ‘red signal.’ They reported that heavy rain was expected within two days and asked that preparations be made. They were told, ‘We will ask those above and tell you.’ No information came. > > “The same information was again supplied by Public Works officials on the morning of the 29th, and again that evening. Still no signal was given. One senior official said, ‘I am about to meet the Chief Minister; I will give you information immediately,’ but supplied none. Even by the Government's own account, Chennai was submerged because so much water was released in a single night.”

The DMK demanded a judicial inquiry so that the truth could be made clear to the people. Because neither an explanation nor an answer was received, the protest is being held in Chennai on 5 January. It is the duty of every one of you to participate. I am confident that you will. Let us meet at the demonstration!

*Junior Vikatan* also published an article describing how the Chief Ministers of Odisha and Andhra Pradesh acted when similar floods and cyclones struck those States in 2013. It carried photographs of both Chief Ministers standing knee-deep in water among affected people and speaking with them.
Writing particularly about my friend and Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu, it said:

> “Chandrababu Naidu arrived in Visakhapatnam from Hyderabad and visited every affected area without omission. He met all officials at the District Collector's office and accelerated relief work. There was not even a suitable place for him to stay. Because Government guest houses had been damaged by the cyclone, he stayed and slept in a bus parked in the Collectorate compound. > > “On the second day, Chandrababu Naidu again inspected cyclone-hit areas. The relief work did not satisfy him. He sternly warned officials and spoke openly about it to journalists.”
Do you know what the same magazine, after writing this about the Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister, said about our Tamil Nadu Chief Minister?

> “Heavy rain fell for more than a month from 28 October across the northern districts of Tamil Nadu, including Chennai and Cuddalore. Weather Centre Director Ramanan went beyond warning of ‘heavy rain’ and even warned of ‘extremely heavy rain.’ Yet even those words did not awaken the Tamil Nadu Government or the Chief Minister from their sleep. No precautionary measures whatsoever were taken. > > “Why were precautions not taken as Odisha Chief Minister Naveen Patnaik and Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu had done? The person who travelled from town to town seeking votes and declaring, ‘Your sister has come,’ what did she do while Chennai's people suffered?”
The ruling party offered no answer to any of this, but the Finance Minister wrote seven pages in reply to a statement issued in Kamal Haasan's name.

When all the neutral newspapers questioned the Tamil Nadu Government in this manner, the Government gave no explanation. That is why the DMK demanded a judicial inquiry to make clear to the people who was responsible. Because neither explanation nor reply was received, the protest demonstration is being held in Chennai on 5 January.
It is the duty of every one of you to participate. I am confident that you will.
Let us meet at the demonstration!
With affection,
M.K.
